theory.canonical_statement: Chapter: The Universality Tests: Quantitative Confirmation of Scale Free Deviation Across Eight Orders of Magnitude Primary Registry IDs: 853 (Lyapunov stability — the deviation bound is the Lyapunov radius), 023 (TZP potential — tested at planetary scale via GRACE/GOCE) Secondary IDs: 010 (stochastic drift — the CMB deviation as relic drift), 0060–0085 (gyromagnetic scaling — the Elsasser criticality underlying the planetary tests) Tertiary IDs: 005 (TZP stability — the universality class as a fixed point) The four tests each have a predicted bound $\epsilon < \epsilon \text{max}$: $10^{ 4}$ for droplets, $10^{ 3}$ for bubbles, $10^{ 2}$ for planets, $\sim 10^{ 5}$ for the CMB. These bounds are not arbitrary — they are the Lyapunov stability radii (ID 853) for each system.
theory.technical_interpretation: class as a fixed point) --- ## The Deviation Bound as the Lyapunov Radius (ID 853) The four tests each have a predicted bound $\epsilon < \epsilon_\text{max}$: $10^{-4}$ for droplets, $10^{-3}$ for bubbles, $10^{-2}$ for planets, $\sim 10^{-5}$ for the CMB. These bounds are not arbitrary — they are the Lyapunov stability radii (ID 853) for each system. The Lyapunov function $V[\Phi] = \|\Phi - \Phi_\text{target}\|^2$ measures the squared distance from the spherical attractor $\Phi_\text{target}$. The deviation $\epsilon$ is the square root of $V$ evaluated at the system's actual state: **A system within its Lyapunov stability radius will return to the spherical attractor.** The bound $\epsilon_\text{max}$ for each system is the maximum deviation from which the system still returns to sphericity rather than fragmenting or destabilising. The water droplet has the tightest bound because its In technical terms, the entry packages the selected formula as the operative carrier, the nearby equations as the progression layer, and the symbol key as the compact interpretation of variables used by the source.
